data.title: Northrop Grumman: The Unrivaled Architect Of The New Nuclear Triad And Orbital Defense
data.description:
Executive Summary & Investment Thesis

Northrop Grumman (NOC) has one differentiation from other defense companies. It doesn't just build tactical products for the United States but also produces highly strategic products. As we are newly beginning another cold war between the United States and China, Northrop is the primary company to carry the nuclear program and space defense systems for the United States. It produces new products for the steps of the nuclear triad.

Currently, many investors miss a point. The B-21 Raider is currently causing problems and a deficit for Northrop's financials. This is because the B-21 is currently in the LRIP stage, and Northrop can't get a profit from this project currently. Therefore, when we look at the financials, the valuation of Northrop is lower than what it actually should be.

The essential point to make Northrop one of the most important defense companies is its space defense system projects and the Sentinel ICBM project. Sentinel is not just a simple project, but it guarantees the future cash flow for Northrop for a long time. This is because it is the essential nuclear program for the United States.

When we consider all these monopolistic points for Northrop, the base DCF value gives us $728.57, and peer valuations give us an $800+ value. So, in my model, I found that Northrop should be more valuable than the current situation.

The New Nuclear Triad: Sentinel and B-21 Raider

The market mostly values defense companies according to how many tactical platforms they provide to the army. However, Northrop is not just a defense company that produces tactical platforms but is also the main contractor of the new nuclear triad. Northrop has monopolistic power in this strategic project, not only for air platforms but also for ground-based platforms. Therefore, it has stable revenue for the future.

The B-21 Raider: Data Cloud in The Sky

Because of this, investors think that the B-21 is just a plane. However, it is the future of the USAF. It is a 6th-gen aircraft that was produced to command all operations thanks to its data-centered structure.

The Financial Reality of LRIP

Currently, the B-21 project is in the LRIP stage, which means that it is costly for Northrop to produce these planes. Investors think that this is the ultimate result of this project; so they have punished Northrop Grumman's stock. However, experienced investors realize that it has just entered into the area that generates high profit for Northrop. When the B-21 starts full-rate production, it will guarantee the next 30 years of cash flow for Northrop. The current situation is just like the travail of a highly strategic project.

The Sentinel Project: The Land-Based Monopoly

The Sentinel project is the land base of this nuclear triad. It is indispensable because it will replace the Minuteman III rockets, which were produced in the 1970s. Northrop Grumman is the only main contractor of this project. The estimated cost of this project is $140 billion.

Many projects may face budget cut issues by the U.S. Congress. However, it is not possible for the Sentinel project because it is a highly strategic project for United States nuclear deterrence. The last budget request from the Pentagon proved this. Even though governments and politics change, this project is indispensable, and the United States has to modernize its ICBM arsenal. Therefore, this case guarantees Northrop Grumman's cash flow for the future.

Conclusion of the Strategic Moat

For this part of the article, these two huge projects are the economic moat that separates Northrop Grumman from other companies. While the market just looks at the short-term financials, Northrop is actually building the United States' nuclear deterrence. So, Northrop is immune to any possible future changes in policy or geopolitical situations.

Because of these macro differences, we can't just judge Northrop Grumman's financials as if they are our only tool. When we try to find the value for Northrop, we should carefully research its space and nuclear projects. The Sentinel and B-21 projects will be the locomotive for Northrop to reach a higher value. These projects are the main points that create Northrop's intrinsic value.

Space Systems: The High-Margin Growth Engine

The New Battlefield

After explaining the B-21 and Sentinel, now I will explain another point that makes Northrop important for the United States' national defense. Space has become a battlefield for all countries. I think we are in the second space race. Therefore, defense companies also have projects for space. And Northrop is one of the most important defense companies for space.

Many people still think that the space race is just for prestige and still only about NASA projects. However, space is a new battlefield now. Defense companies have projects about hypersonic glide vehicles and anti-satellite systems. They also use satellites for early warning about ballistic missiles. So, the space segment is a highly rising segment among others. And Northrop has some crucial space projects as well.

SDA and The Legacy Collapse

The SDA recently changed its strategy about space. In the past, the United States' space defense just relied on big, expensive, and slow satellites. On the other hand, now the United States has changed its doctrine to many small, cheap, and fast-moving satellites for national defense.

Northrop Grumman's Position

In this structural change, Northrop was the leader of the defense companies. Other companies were slow to adapt to this structural shift, while Northrop had already started to develop projects for this change. So, Northrop took the biggest piece of the pie for the Tranche 1 and Tranche 2 projects, which are about the Tracking and Transport Layer. Consequently, Northrop is not just a regular defense company but the main producer of the military internet in orbit.

The Backlog Narrative

We can clearly see this advantage in Northrop Grumman's financials. Northrop Grumman has a $95.68 billion backlog as of the end of 2025. $26.2 billion of this backlog belongs to the space segment. This amount is even bigger than many defense companies' total values. And another point is that the space segment is growing much faster than other segments. So, the space segment is the engine of growth for Northrop Grumman.

Margin Expansion

So, what is the meaning of all this data? We now see that space is our future. So, if the space segment becomes more important compared to other segments in a company, we can say that this company has a competitive advantage over others. In the end, we can conclude that cash flow will increase in the future for Northrop Grumman.

Concise Valuation Analysis: The Price of a Strategic Monopoly

Now, I will explain the most important point of this analysis. I used three different methods to find the fair value of Northrop Grumman. These are DCF, DDM, and relative valuation. I used three different scenarios for both DCF and DDM. But I will explain only the base scenario for both of these methods to avoid making this part too long and complex. Also, I used two different approaches in the relative valuation method. These are the average of the companies and the market cap weighted average of the companies. I think that we can find a more correct value if we use the market cap weighted average of the companies; so, I will explain that method. However, I have all the methods and their applications in my Excel model.

Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)

As you can see in the detailed WACC calculation table, I found the WACC to be 7.09% and the growth rate to be 3.50%. When I applied the DCF model in my base case scenario, I found the fair value of Northrop to be $728.57, which represents a 6.94% upside potential as of the market close on April 13, 2026. This value suggests a BUY or HOLD rating. I apply the method assuming that past performance will reflect the future. While I don't think past performance will directly reflect the future, I accept this to create a base scenario for the DCF model.

Dividend Discount Model (DDM)

When I applied the DDM to find the fair value of Northrop Grumman, I found the fair value of Northrop Grumman to be $680.65, which is a 0.09% downside as of the market close on April 13, 2026. As we can see, we found the current market price of Northrop when we applied this method. On the other hand, I think the same as I already explained in the DCF method. These methods can't show the real value of Northrop Grumman because they only focus on financials.

Relative Valuation

The last method I applied is relative valuation. I explained the market cap weighted average in this part. When we applied this method to find the fair value using the EV/EBITDA ratio, the fair value of Northrop Grumman was $869.42, which represents a 27.61% upside potential as of the market close on April 13, 2026. Another ratio we can use is the P/E ratio. When we use this ratio to find the fair value of Northrop Grumman, we find this value to be $988.04, which has a 45.02% upside potential as of the market close on April 13, 2026. The last ratio I used to find the fair value of Northrop Grumman is the EV/Sales ratio. When we apply this ratio to find the fair value of Northrop Grumman, we find this value to be $828.25, which is a 21.56% upside potential as of the market close on April 13, 2026.

When we apply all three of these methods, we mostly see that the fair value of Northrop Grumman should be higher than the current market price. Therefore, we can give a BUY rating when we consider all of the financial methods that were used to find the fair value of the company.

Targeted & Business-Specific Risks

Even though Northrop has many advantages over the market, there are still some risks that may hurt Northrop Grumman's value in the future. Northrop Grumman is a huge defense company. So, macroeconomic changes may affect Northrop badly. But some specific risks may hurt Northrop Grumman more than others.

Fixed-Price Contract Risks

This is actually the biggest risk for defense companies because governments are the main customers of defense companies. Therefore, when they sign a contract, they don't change how much money they will pay, even if all the prices change in the world and the cost of the products increases. The same point applies to Northrop Grumman. The B-21 Raider is the biggest project of Northrop Grumman now. If the cost of the B-21 increases, the profit of Northrop Grumman will decrease. And the current geopolitical situation is increasing this risk. The conflict between the USA and Iran, and also China's new policy, increase the risk of any shortages of materials. So, this is the primary risk for Northrop Grumman.

The "New Space" Competition

Even though Northrop Grumman started the space race earlier than many companies, there are many companies trying to take a slice of the pie. Another problem for Northrop is that even though it can decide faster than many other big companies, there are many small companies that can decide much faster and are braver than Northrop. So, in the future, Northrop may lose its leading position in this industry.

Conclusion

When we think about all of the financial and strategic analyses, we can reach a conclusion about the fair value of Northrop Grumman. In my opinion, when I consider all of these, Northrop Grumman is not just a Hold stock but a Buy stock. Many investors still haven't realized that the point of the B-21 is just a temporary cost for Northrop and will give a huge profit in the future. Because this is one leg of the U.S. nuclear triad, even if the policy and government change in the United States, they can't cancel this program.

Other than the B-21, Northrop is also the main producer of the ground-based leg of the nuclear triad. The Sentinel project is the ground-based part of the nuclear triad. So, Northrop Grumman has a monopoly on the nuclear triad of the United States. This position guarantees a long-term cash flow for Northrop Grumman and shows us how important it is for the national defense of the United States.

Even though the DCF model gives us a little upside potential, I think that this doesn't reflect all the potential of Northrop Grumman. When we look at the relative valuation, we can see the real potential of Northrop Grumman in a better way. However, I think that Northrop's value is more than just financial because its main value comes from its strategic importance.

For investors who invest for the long term, Northrop Grumman has great potential for the long run. Because Northrop is not just a defense company, it is an indispensable company for the national defense of the United States and the monopolistic power of the nuclear triad. So, for investors who invest for the long term, it is a rational choice for their portfolio.

data.title: Nissan Motor: Favorable Read-Across From Latest Investor Event
data.description:
I've stayed bullish on Nissan Motor Co., Ltd. (NSANY) (NSANF) (7201.T).

It is expected to record a revenue decline and net losses for FY2025 (YE Mar 2026). But I'm optimistic about its future. NSANY organized a presentation outlining its "long-term vision" on April 14. These disclosures have left me with a positive impression of its financial improvement potential.

My prior March 21, 2024, article touched on the firm's inorganic expansion and China prospects.

Anticipating A Growth Turnaround

NSANY aims to register combined unit sales of 2.55 million for the U.S./China/Japan regions in FY30. This figure was taken from the S&P Capital IQ transcript for Tuesday's event.

The new volume target's implied forward five-year CAGR is +4%. That represents a reversal from the group's FY25 guided -2% volume decline in the same markets.

I have confidence in NSANY's ability to perform better for a number of reasons.

The company indicated at the April 14 webcast that it "will increase (future) volume per model by more than 30%." I think this is achievable, considering its proposal to pivot towards a more focused portfolio.

Going forward, NSANY intends to have just a few core categories accounting for about four-fifths of total volumes. One of them is the "Compact Family" catering to demand for smaller-sized vehicles in Japan. Also, the total nameplate count is planned to be decreased by 19% to 45.

I believe that the enterprise's top line can be enhanced as top executives spend less time managing an excessive number of product lines.

Separately, NSANY's Tuesday briefing comments suggest that it "will equip the vast majority (90%) of its lineup with AI-driven technologies" over time.

In my opinion, it is the right decision to place a greater emphasis on AI-related features. Deloitte's "2026 Global Automotive Study" highlighted that "consumers in most markets view software-defined vehicles (SDVs) as valuable." The proportion is among the highest globally in China at "68%." This is a critical region for NSANY, as mentioned above.

The firm has dealt with a major pain point as well. It has been too slow in capitalizing on driver preference changes in the past. At the mid-April call, NSANY disclosed that its "time-to-market was reduced" to two and a half years.

The sell-side is currently projecting that Nissan's turnover will go up 3.5%, 2.8%, and 3.8% for the FY26-28 periods, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ. My take is that its actual revenue rise could be closer to a mid-single-digit percentage, paralleling the volume goals.

The Company Should Continue To Get Leaner

SA analyst "Caffital Research" drew attention to NSANY's "Re:Nissan plan unveiled in May" involving a "significant restructuring to fix the cost base" in an Oct 10, 2025 piece. I'm positively surprised that the group's internal reorganization is ahead of schedule.

It was initially targeting a ¥0.15 trillion cut in non-variable expenses by the end of FY25, or March 31, 2026. But NSANY had already realized ¥160 billion of savings as of 3QFY2025. This led to the full-year guide being lifted to ¥0.20 trillion. Management attributed this to the speedy "consolidation of 7 vehicle production sites within 10 months of the Re:Nissan launch" at the mid-Feb earnings call.

In my view, its profitability outlook looks promising, judging by the April 14 disclosures.

NSANY outlined its engineering "Key Performance Indicators/KPIs" of a "20% reduction in part count and 70% decrease in parts complexity" at the latest presentation.

Also, there's a strong intent to minimize the unfavorable effects of import duties. Its goal is to raise the onshoring ratio for U.S. businesses by 20 ppts to "80%" in due course.

In the preceding section, I wrote about NSANY's portfolio rationalization efforts to bring its total number of models to the mid-40s level. I would assume that companywide margins should widen with the removal of certain subscale and unprofitable cars. At the "Long-Term Vision" event, the enterprise has clearly stated its aim of manufacturing at or close to full capacity in the future.

Management is forecasting a -¥0.65 trillion loss for full-year FY25. But I see it being in the black again soon in the next fiscal year, thanks to its "lean" mentality.

You Should Weigh These Bear-Case Scenarios

External headwinds like elevated energy prices and stiffer competition could prevent NSANY from realizing its '30 ambitions.

The firm might execute poorly on its cost control measures, leading to a longer-than-expected time to turn profitable again.

My Concluding Remarks

I'm keeping NSANY as a "Buy"-rated name.

My target price-to-book for this stock is 0.33 times using the "Gordon Growth Model/GGM" formula. I assume a 3% ROE (FY2027 consensus), a 7% equity cost (KPMG estimate), and a +1% perpetuity growth (developed economy proxy).

This implies that its shares could appreciate by +27% (0.33/0.26), considering the current P/B of 0.26x.

The key catalyst is a return to positive topline and bottom-line territory, in my view.

data.title: Our Take On The Current Private Credit Environment
data.description:
Recent headlines have raised questions about private credit, particularly around credit stress, software-related exposure, and the flexibility of illiquid investment vehicles in meeting redemption requests.

Those concerns warrant attention, but they do not apply uniformly across private credit strategies or structures. In our view, the current environment reinforces the importance of strategy design, manager discipline, and portfolio construction.

What has been happening in markets

Recent market commentary has generally focused on a few key areas:

  • More visible credit losses and defaults. A handful of higher-profile defaults and losses, including a narrow group involving alleged fraud, have put a spotlight on underwriting quality, borrower fundamentals, recovery outcomes, and how consistently private loans are being valued.
  • Software exposure and AI-related concerns. Concerns around lending within the software industry have grown as investors reassess the earnings durability of borrowing companies given the potential for AI-driven disruption across parts of the industry.
  • Liquidity pressure in vehicles with limited liquidity. Increased redemption requests within certain funds have reminded investors of the liquidity constraints that can exist in private markets, especially during periods of stress.

Against that backdrop, the following discussion focuses on several considerations that are particularly relevant in the current environment.

Understanding liquidity in interval fund vehicles

Liquidity has been one of the central concerns in recent coverage of the asset class. Much of that attention has centered on redemption requests exceeding the amount that funds offer to repurchase in a given period. Elevated redemptions can create negative headlines, but they are not necessarily evidence that a fund is failing to operate as designed.

Investors allocate to private credit in large part to earn an illiquidity premium and seek higher income potential than is typically available in more liquid public markets. In return, liquidity is more limited and governed by transparent repurchase terms. Those limits help managers meet redemption requests in an orderly manner without being forced into rapid sales of generally illiquid underlying loans at potentially depressed prices.

Recent events show that private credit, like any asset class, is not immune to redemption pressure. Even so, it may be among the better-suited private market asset classes for structures that offer limited, periodic repurchase features, given the typically shorter duration of underlying assets, consistent income generation, and healthy organic turnover through repayments and amortization.

Many interval funds offer quarterly repurchases of 5% of outstanding shares under a clearly defined framework disclosed to investors in advance. That is also the structure we chose for the Callodine Specialty Income Fund (Class I: CALIX).

The fund’s asset allocation framework also includes public market income sleeves, which can provide a liquidity buffer alongside the private credit portfolio. In our view, that design helps balance reasonable client liquidity with the ability to invest in less liquid credit markets in pursuit of attractive income and return potential.

Leverage across product structures

Leverage is another area that has drawn increased attention in recent headlines, particularly amid concerns about the health of underlying loans in some parts of the market. Put simply, leverage is the use of borrowed capital to increase investment exposure. It can enhance returns when credit performs well, but it can also magnify losses when conditions become more difficult.

Leverage usage differs meaningfully across vehicles, with business development companies (BDCs) generally offering the greatest flexibility. Interval funds operate within a more conservative framework, but even within the interval fund universe, there is a wide range of leverage usage depending on manager approach. Some funds use leverage more aggressively, while others use little or none at all.

Ultimately, leverage is neither inherently positive nor negative. It is one of several structural choices that can shape a fund’s return profile and sensitivity to downside risk. CALIX was designed to operate without leverage. While that may not maximize headline yield, we believe it can help reduce downside potential and support a more measured and consistent risk/reward profile.

Software exposure and manager differentiation

Software exposure has become a focal point in private credit commentary, particularly within traditional direct lending. That concern is understandable, as software-related borrowers represent a sizable share of the direct lending market, and AI has raised legitimate questions about the durability of some software business models.

At the same time, exposure can vary meaningfully depending on manager approach, strategy mix, and portfolio construction. Investors should also recognize that credit risk may evolve differently than equity risk, particularly when loan maturities are shorter than the timeline over which AI disruption may play out.

Within the interval fund universe, portfolios tend to be diversified across multiple credit strategies and investment approaches, rather than concentrated solely in direct middle-market lending, let alone software. That makes it important not to extrapolate the potential risk within the software space too broadly across the asset class.

CALIX is a diversified solution, with direct lending representing one of several private credit strategies within the broader portfolio. Within the direct lending segment, software has not been a primary area of investment. More broadly, the fund employs dedicated strategies across asset-based lending, real estate lending, life sciences, aviation lending, entertainment lending, high-yield public bonds, and yielding public equities.

Many of the underlying private credit investments within these strategies tend to be shorter duration, often in the two- to three-year range, and structured to repay principal over time rather than relying primarily on a large repayment at maturity. Together, those characteristics can support diversified income generation and create a more natural source of portfolio liquidity through ongoing repayments.

Conclusion

In our view, recent headlines have not called into question the role of private credit broadly, but they have reinforced the importance of selectivity within the asset class. Ultimately, every asset class carries risk. Even traditional safe-haven segments such as broad public fixed income have at times struggled to generate adequate returns, including over the last five years.

The case for private credit is not that it is risk-free, but that it can continue to serve a useful role in diversified portfolios, particularly when accessed through a structure with clear liquidity parameters, limited structural complexity, and diversified sources of income.

These considerations were central to the design of CALIX, and we remain confident that our approach is well suited to help clients achieve their investment objectives across a range of market and economic conditions, regardless of the latest financial media headlines.
